data.title: Google taps AI to launch hurricane prediction website as above-average 2025 season begins
data.description:
Google Deepmind (NASDAQ:GOOG)(NASDAQ:GOOGL) and Google Research have partnered with the U.S. National Hurricane Center to launch a new, interactive website focused on tropical cyclone predictions.

The new website, Weather Lab, utilizes Google's latest artificial intelligence weather models, including its experimental AI-based tropical cyclone model, based on stochastic neural networks. 

"This model can predict a cyclone's formation, track, intensity, size and shape — generating 50 possible scenarios, up to 15 days ahead," Google said.

"Internal testing shows that our model's predictions for cyclone track and intensity are as accurate as, and often more accurate than, current physics-based methods," Google added.

Weather Lab users can compare predictions from various AI and physics-based models. 

It is a timely release, as the hurricane season officially began on June 1 and will end on November 30. Multiple research institutions and agencies expect an above-average hurricane season this year.

"A warmer-than-normal tropical Atlantic combined with likely ENSO (El Niño Southern Oscillation) neutral conditions typically provides a more conducive dynamic and thermodynamic environment for hurricane formation and intensification," according to Colorado State University's June 11 forecast. "We anticipate an above-average probability for major hurricanes making landfall along the continental United States coastline and in the Caribbean."

This echoes a similar forecast by the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration. NOAA's outlook expects a 60% chance of an above-normal season with 13 to 19 named storms. This includes three to five hurricanes ranking category 3 or higher.

The CSU forecasts calls for 17 named storms and four major hurricanes. The average number of named storms from 1991 to 2020 was 14.4 with 3.2 major hurricanes.

At the same time, U.S. President Donald Trump's administration is seeking to slash NOAA funding by $1.3B, or about 25%.

"The Budget terminates a variety of climate-dominated research, data, and grant programs, which are not aligned with Administration policy-ending 'Green New Deal' initiatives," reads the Fiscal Year 2026 Discretionary Budget Request. "For example, NOAA's educational grant programs have consistently funded efforts to radicalize students against markets and spread environmental alarm."

"Proposed budget cuts will impact NOAA's core functions, including ocean monitoring, regional and coastal planning, climate research, and weather forecasting," according to the Council on Foreign Relations.

data.title: Mortgage rates down marginally, moving within a narrow range for the past few months
data.description:
Mortgage rates were slightly down this week, moving within a narrow range for the past few months, according to the Freddie Mac Primary Mortgage Survey.

30-year fixed-rate mortgages averaged 6.84% as of June 12, down marginally from 6.85% last week and compared to 6.95% in the year-ago week.

15-year fixed-rate mortgages averaged 5.97%, down from 5.99% in the prior week and 6.17% a year ago.

"Mortgage rates have moved within a narrow range for the past few months and this week is no different," said Sam Khater, Freddie Mac's chief economist.

"Rate stability, improving inventory and slower house price growth are an encouraging combination as we celebrate National Homeownership Month," added Khater.

data.title: CN to invest $30M in Tennessee
data.description:
• CN (NYSE:CNI) said on Thursday that it plans to invest approximately US$30 million in Tennessee, as part of its 2025 capital investment program. 
  • This includes investments in infrastructure, technology, rolling stock equipment, as well as network improvements.

data.title: Least shorted S&P500 stocks in May
data.description:
Medtronic (MDT) was the least shorted S&P 500 (SP500) stock as a percentage of float in May, with 0.62% of its shares sold short.
 
The medical device maker recently raised its dividend payment, marking 48 consecutive years of growth in payouts and increasing its dividend yield further above 3%, more than double the benchmark index’s sub-1.5% yield. However, the Ireland-based med-tech set its fiscal 2026 earnings guidance below expectations despite posting a double beat with its Q4 FY25 financials.
 
Alphabet (GOOG)(GOOGL) had a short interest of 0.63% of its float in May, with shares rising around 11% in the past month. The tech giant touted its AI investments at its annual developer conference last month, stating that it has released more than a dozen AI models and 20 features since last year's conference.
 
Apple (AAPL) was the third least shorted S&P 500 stock in May, with a short interest representing 0.64% of the float. The iPhone maker’s shares dropped 5% in the past month, during which it reported fiscal second-quarter results that topped estimates and increased its return to shareholders, including a new $100B share buyback program.
 
Here are the top 20 least shorted S&P 500 stocks as a percentage of float, as of May:

data.title: Trump blasts Fed boss Powell as 'numbskull' over rate-cut resistance
data.description:
U.S. President Donald Trump on Thursday called Fed Chair Jerome Powell a "numbskull" for keeping interest rates on hold and not cutting. 

At the White House, Trump contended that reducing rates by two percentage points would result in $600B in annual savings for the U.S.

“We’re going to spend $600 billion a year, $600 billion because of one numbskull that sits here [saying] ‘I don’t see enough reason to cut the rates now,’” Trump said during a bill signing ceremony.

It's one thing if inflation was accelerating, in which case the Fed might need to raise rates, Trump said. But inflation is cooling, he added, “and I may have to force something.”

Trump's remarks come just a day after he called on the central bank to slash borrowing costs by a full percentage point, citing May's softer-than-expected CPI data.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: ProShares launches two new ETFs
data.description:
ETFs provider ProShares announced on Thursday the launch of two new exchange-traded funds, ProShares Ultra Top QQQ (NASDAQ:QQUP) and ProShares UltraShort Top QQQ (NASDAQ:QQDN).

QQUP targets 2x daily returns of the Nasdaq-100 Mega Index, while QQDN targets -2x daily returns of the same index.

data.title: Consumer income growth can help economy avoid recession- J.P. Morgan
data.description:
Consumer income growth should help the U.S. economy to avoid recession, J.P. Morgan said in its midyear economic outlook.

"Our 2025 year-ahead outlook built off two propositions: momentum in the business cycle at the turn of the year was solid, and policy risks were bidirectional," Michael Feroli, analyst at J.P. Morgan, said in a note. 

J.P. Morgan added that in the next six months, the solid momentum has continued, with stable growth in jobs and spending. "We have gotten more clarity on policy it has been a mixed bag, as expected, but we think tilting in a less growth-friendly way."

But policy has also given some growth-supportive offsets, including a boost in Social Security payments, which has lifted personal income, offsetting some of the higher tax burden of tariffs, Feroli added.

Feroli also said personal incomes should get some support from President Donald Trump's One Big Beautiful Bill making its way through Congress, which J.P. Morgan expects will be passed in modified form before the August recess.

"These offsets should limit the risk that higher tariffs drag the economy into recession. Even so, we view recession risks as still elevated and see about a one in three chance the economy slips into contraction in the coming four quarters," Feroli said.

The capital spending will be hurt by ongoing uncertainty about global trading rules, said J.P. Morgan, adding, "Finally, U.S. policy is weighing on the global growth outlook, which in turn will depress foreign demand for U.S. exports."

Feroli also believes there will be a slowdown in the number of monthly job growth, as recent benchmark revisions suggested that this slowing has been happening sooner than expected.

"For the remainder of the year, the inflation story is likely to be centered around tariffs. Through May, there is scant evidence that tariffs are already being felt in a big way in consumer prices," Feroli said. 

J.P. Morgan added the cooling inflation trends are likely to heat back up this summer, and the Federal Reserve is expected to wait until December for the next rate cut.

data.title: Teck Resources, Sumitomo at odds over copper supply deal, exposing cracks in pricing system
data.description:
Canada's Teck Resources (NYSE:TECK) and Japan's Sumitomo Metal Mining (OTCPK:SMMYY) are locked in a commercial dispute over a major copper supply deal, exposing cracks in a decades-old pricing system as the market endures a shortage, Bloomberg reported Thursday.

The two sides have failed to agree on the sales terms for concentrated copper ores that Teck (NYSE:TECK) will supply to Sumitomo’s (OTCPK:SMMYY) smelter in Japan this year, and the dispute centers on the value of treatment and refining charges that will be deducted from the price of Teck's concentrates to cover Sumitomo’s smelting costs, according to the report.

TC/RC fees have plummeted to record lows during the past year due to a surge in global smelting capacity that has created stiff competition for mined ores.

The global copper industry has long relied on a single benchmark TC/RC set by major miners and smelters, with Chilean miner Antofagasta's (OTC:ANFGF) deals providing the reference for this year, and shifts in the benchmark have major implications for smelters' margins, with TC/RCs typically accounting for a third of their incomes, Wood Mackenzie said.

Antofagasta (OTC:ANFGF) and Chinese smelters struck a supply deal for 2025 late last year that set TC/RCs at $21.25/ton of ore processed and 2.125 cents/lb of refined metal produced, which has been widely adopted as a benchmark.

But Antofagasta (OTC:ANFGF) later signed smaller contracts with some Japanese buyers carrying higher TC/RCs of ~$25/2.5 cents, causing Sumitomo (OTCPK:SMMYY) and some other smelters to resist using $21.25/2.125 as their contractual reference, creating strains in commercial negotiations and reigniting a debate about the fairness of the benchmark system.

ETFs: (CPER), (COPX), (OTC:JJCTF)

data.title: Fed rate cut expectations rise on soft inflation data, signs of labor market cooling
data.description:
Market participants on Thursday pushed up their expectations for Federal Reserve interest rate cuts this year, following two sets of benign inflation data this week and some signs of cooling in the labor market.

Since cutting interest rates by 100 basis points at the end of 2024, the Fed has stuck to a wait-and-see approach as U.S. President Donald Trump's tariffs have clouded economic growth expectations. Though inflation has trended in the right direction, continued strength in the labor market has also kept the Fed on hold.     

This week's softer-than-anticipated consumer price index (CPI) and producer price index (PPI) reports for May have shown little effects from tariffs.

"With (Wednesday’s) highly anticipated CPI print coming in below expectations on MoM and core readings for its fourth month in a row, inflation has proven to be tamer and more controlled than initially expected," Rick Rieder, CIO of global fixed income at BlackRock, said on X (formerly Twitter) yesterday.  

Meanwhile, last week's nonfarm payrolls report showed a M/M moderation in May and significant revisions to job growth for March and April. 

"While tariffs could still present upside risks to inflation, (Wednesday’s) print along with a solid, but moderating labor market (highlighted by last week’s payrolls), significantly increases the likelihood of Fed cuts this year, particularly if there is material slowing in employment," Rieder added.  

On Thursday, data from the U.S. Department of Labor showed claims for insured employment - also known as continuing claims - for the week ending May 31 hitting its highest level since November 2021. 

"Labor market cooling: Seasonally adjusted initial claims for unemployment benefits held at a seven-month high last week. Without a seasonal adjustment, claims were running ahead of last year but were below the 2023 level. Continuing claims are at a four-year high," Nick Timiraos, the Wall Street Journal's Fed watcher, said on X. 

The current federal funds rate stands at 4.25%-4.50%. According to the CME FedWatch tool, the odds of that rate falling to 3.50%-3.75% in December now stands at 28.8% from 24.8% a day ago, and the odds of that rate falling to 3.25%-3.50% has inched up to 5.7% from 3.9% the previous day.

The Fed is in its blackout period this week, ahead of its fourth monetary policy committee meeting of the year on June 17 and 18. Traders almost unanimously expect policymakers to take no action on rates.

"Changes to trade policy have ushered in upside risks to both unemployment and inflation. But, with current data showing activity remaining resilient, we expect the post-meeting statement and Chair Powell's press conference will convey that the Committee is still in no hurry to adjust policy," Wells Fargo's Jay Bryson said. 

"We believe the FOMC will need to see more pronounced softening in the labor market to begin cutting the fed funds rate again this year. We expect to see this softening in the coming months and look for 75 bps of easing by year end," Bryson added.

Thursday's soft PPI data sparked a rally in bonds, while equities fluctuated amid trade and geopolitical tensions. Here are some exchange-traded funds that track the benchmark S&P 500 index (SP500): (NYSEARCA:SPY), (NYSEARCA:VOO), (NYSEARCA:IVV), (NYSEARCA:RSP), (NYSEARCA:SSO), (NYSEARCA:UPRO), (NYSEARCA:SH), (NYSEARCA:SDS), and (NYSEARCA:SPXU).

data.title: AppLovin drops as Culper Research says it's short
data.description:
• AppLovin (NASDAQ:APP) shares fell 5% on Thursday after Culper Research said it's short shares of the technology firm.
  • “We are short AppLovin ... because we believe the Company has blatantly misrepresented the scope of both its Chinese shareholders and its Chinese operations, posing not only risks to shareholders, but national security,” Culper Research wrote in its report.
  • Culper added that it believes AppLovin has been backed by Chinese national Hao Tang through a variety of offshore shell companies, with him ultimately controlling at least 9.8% of Class A shares. 
  • AppLovin has repeatedly denied the claims of Chinese ownership and ties to China.
  • AppLovin did not immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.
